misconception: Tannins and acidity both taste 'sharp' so they are the same thing.
why_incorrect: Tannins and acidity are chemically and sensorially distinct components. Acidity is perceived as a sharp, mouth-watering sensation on the sides of the tongue and is caused by hydrogen ions (low pH). Tannins create astringency — a drying, gripping sensation across the gums and inner cheeks caused by polyphenols binding to salivary proteins. A wine can have high tannin and low acidity (e.g., some warm-climate reds), or high acidity and low tannin (e.g., dry Riesling). Confusing these leads to systematic errors in SAT assessments.
corrected_understanding: Acidity = mouth-watering sourness on the tongue sides (hydrogen ions). Tannins = drying, gripping astringency on gums and cheeks (polyphenols binding saliva proteins). They are independent structural components.
detection_signals: both tannin and acidity make the wine sharp
I can't tell the difference between tannic and acidic
tannin is just another word for acidic

coaching_content.confusion_statement: You seem to be confusing: Tannins and acidity both taste 'sharp' so they are the same thing.
coaching_content.evidence_statement: This is based on your answers showing this pattern.
coaching_content.improvement_signal: Correctly explaining the relationship in similar questions
